questionText: The time, $X$ minutes, that customers wait to be served at a help desk is modelled by $X \sim N(8, 2.5^2)$. Find the waiting time that is exceeded by $15\%$ of customers, giving your answer to $3$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and the condition
steps.explanation: Waiting times are normal with mean $\mu = 8$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 2.5$. ‘Exceeded by $15\%$ of customers’ means $15\%$ of waits are longer than the value $a$, so $P(X > a) = 0.15$.
Inverse-normal works from the area to the left, so we rewrite the condition: if $15\%$ wait longer than $a$, then $85\%$ wait less than $a$, giving $P(X < a) = 0.85$.
The standardised value with $85\%$ of the area to its left is $z = 1.036$. It is positive because $a$ sits above the mean — only the shorter, more common waits fall below it.
Standardising the unknown time $a$ must give the $z$-value we just found. Setting the two equal ties the actual waiting time to the known percentile.
Multiplying both sides by $\sigma$ and adding $\mu$ makes $a$ the subject. This is the general rule for turning a $z$-value back into an $x$-value in the original units.
Putting in the mean $8$, the standard deviation $2.5$ and the $z$-value $1.036$ gives an expression for the waiting time in minutes.
Carrying out the arithmetic gives a waiting time of about $10.59$ minutes.

steps.description: State the answer to 3 significant figures
steps.explanation: Rounding gives the waiting time exceeded by $15\%$ of customers.
The answer lies above the mean of $8$ minutes, which is exactly what we expect: only a minority ($15\%$) of customers wait longer than a time that is already above average.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $a \approx 10.6$ minutes (3 s.f.) — the waiting time exceeded by $15\%$ of customers.
